docs.title: Geo-CAD Practical ReferenceSimple, Practical, and Field-Focused
docs.text:
Learn high-impact CAD, spreadsheet, and GIS workflows in one connected reference.

AutoCAD Civil 3D 2021 • Excel 365 • QGIS 3.40 • Google Earth Pro • OneDrive

This documentation is written for beginner-to-intermediate users who need clear theory and practical execution steps.

docs.title: What This Reference Covers
docs.text:
• Plain-language definitions for key concepts.
  • Context for when to use each tool.
  • Practical, step-by-step workflows.
  • Pros, cons, and recommendations.
  • Common industry standards and format practices.
  • Interoperability between CSV, CAD, GIS, and KML or KMZ.

docs.title: Reference Scope
docs.text:
• Building example: two-room, ground-floor plan.
  • Site context: one simple approach road.
  • Survey input: total station CSV points.
  • GIS input: AOI, basemap, DEM, contours.

docs.title: End-to-End Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
        A[Total Station CSV] --> B[Excel Cleaning and Checks]
        B --> C[AutoCAD Drafting and Dimensions]
        A --> D[QGIS Point Layer]
        D --> E[DEM and Contour Processing]
        E --> F[Map Layout Export]
        D --> G[KML or KMZ]
        C --> H[GIS Export and Import]
        G --> I[Google Earth Pro Visualization]

docs.title: Recommended Reading Path
docs.text:
1. Start with concepts and standards.
  2. Learn each tool reference page.
  3. Practice the integrated execution guide.
  4. Use the cheatsheet while doing tasks.
  5. Use troubleshooting when outputs mismatch.

docs.title: Screenshot Placeholder
docs.text: Insert screenshot: final project output folder with CAD drawing, PDF, Excel workbook, GeoTIFF, contour layer, and KML file.

docs.title: AutoCAD Civil 3D Reference
docs.text: This page explains high-impact Civil 3D drafting and interoperability operations in simple language.

docs.title: What AutoCAD Civil 3D Is
docs.text:
Definition: AutoCAD Civil 3D is a CAD platform used to create, edit, and present engineering drawings and civil design elements.

Context: It is used where geometry clarity, drafting speed, and plotting quality matter.

Typical use cases: - Building plan drafting. - Section and elevation preparation. - Road alignment context drawing. - CAD and GIS data exchange.

docs.title: Why It Is Useful
docs.text:
Pros: - Precise drafting control. - Strong layer and property management. - Industry-accepted drawing outputs. - Good interoperability with GIS through map tools.

Cons: - Learning curve for new users. - Wrong units or CRS setup can create major location errors. - Over-detailed styling can slow practical work.

Recommendation: Focus first on geometry quality, dimensions, and clean layers. Styling can be refined later.

docs.title: Core Drafting Concepts
docs.text:
• Geometry should be clean and connected.
  • Closed boundaries help area, hatch, and export quality.
  • Layer discipline keeps files readable and reusable.
  • Object snaps improve precision and reduce rework.

docs.title: High-Impact Commands
docs.text:
Selection and navigation: - Different object selection methods. - Zoom extents. - Zoom window. - Pan. - Regen.

Drawing and editing: - Polyline. - Closed polyline. - Circle. - Erase. - Move. - Copy. - Rotate. - Scale with reference. - Trim. - Offset. - Mirror. - Boundary. - Extend. - Explode. - Join. - Pedit.

Standards and readability: - Layer panel. - Properties panel. - Match properties. - Snap. - Osnap. - Dimensions.

GIS interoperability commands: - mapimport. - mapiinsert. - mapexport.

docs.title: Step-by-Step Practical Workflow
docs.text: Use the simple sample: two-room ground-floor building with approach road context.

docs.title: Part A: Build the Plan
docs.text:
1. Draw the outer footprint using polyline.
  2. Draw room partition lines.
  3. Make required boundaries closed.
  4. Add circles or symbols only where needed.

docs.title: Part B: Convert to Practical Wall Plan
docs.text:
1. Use offset for wall thickness.
  2. Use trim and extend to clean intersections.
  3. Use erase to remove extra segments.
  4. Use join or pedit where continuity is required.

docs.title: Part C: Add Openings and Presentation
docs.text:
1. Mark door and window positions.
  2. Cut openings with trim and extend.
  3. Add dimensions.
  4. Add annotations.
  5. Keep linework readable at print scale.

docs.title: Part D: Prepare Output
docs.text:
1. Create one layout.
  2. Set viewport and scale.
  3. Verify lineweights and text size.
  4. Export to PDF.

docs.title: Import GIS vectors
docs.text:
1. Use mapimport.
  2. Select input format, such as shapefile.
  3. Map layer and attribute settings.
  4. Check geometry location after import.

docs.title: Insert georeferenced raster
docs.text:
1. Use mapiinsert.
  2. Select georeferenced basemap GeoTIFF.
  3. Confirm scale and placement.

docs.title: Export GIS-ready output
docs.text:
1. Use mapexport.
  2. Select required format.
  3. Keep useful attributes.
  4. Validate export in QGIS.

docs.title: Industry Practices
docs.text:
• Keep base drawing units in meters for field workflows.
  • Use clear layer naming conventions.
  • Avoid exploding blocks unless required.
  • Keep one clean master file and export working copies.
  • Record CRS assumptions when exchanging with GIS users.

docs.title: Recommendations for Beginner to Intermediate Users
docs.text:
• Do not over-customize settings early.
  • Use default styles first and improve only critical output elements.
  • Validate geometry before adding detailed annotations.
  • Check print preview before final PDF.

Insert screenshot: line plan transformed into wall plan with openings.

Insert screenshot: mapimport dialog for shapefile import.

docs.title: Cheatsheet
docs.text: This page is for quick recall while doing practical work.

docs.title: CRS Cheatsheet
docs.text:
• EPSG:4326: latitude and longitude for exchange and KML workflows.
  • EPSG:3857: web map display CRS.
  • EPSG:32643: projected metric CRS for analysis and measurements in this reference.

docs.title: AutoCAD Civil 3D Cheatsheet
docs.text:
Navigation and selection: - Object selection methods. - Zoom extents. - Zoom window. - Pan. - Regen.

Draw and edit: - Polyline. - Closed polyline. - Circle. - Erase. - Move. - Copy. - Rotate. - Scale with reference. - Trim. - Offset. - Mirror. - Boundary. - Extend. - Explode. - Join. - Pedit.

Control and output: - Layer panel. - Properties panel. - Match properties. - Snap and osnap. - Dimensions. - mapimport. - mapiinsert. - mapexport.

docs.title: Excel 365 Cheatsheet
docs.text: Core operations: - Basic math operations. - round, mround, ceil, floor. - index-match. - Duplicate and unique checks. - Find and replace. - Table, filter, sort, and stats. - Page setup.

docs.title: QGIS Cheatsheet
docs.text:
Core steps: - Set project CRS. - Add layers. - Check layer properties. - Reproject with Warp GDAL. - Extract contours with GDAL Contour. - Export map layout.

Plugins: - QuickWKT. - QuickMapServices. - Advanced Map Downloader. - OpenTopography DEM Downloader. - Profile Tool. - Road Slope Calculator.

docs.title: Interoperability Cheatsheet
docs.text:
• CSV to Excel for cleaning and formulas.
  • CSV to QGIS for spatial point loading.
  • QGIS vector to CAD via mapimport.
  • QGIS GeoTIFF to CAD via mapiinsert.
  • CAD to GIS via mapexport.
  • GIS to Google Earth Pro via KML or KMZ.

docs.title: Core Concepts and Standards
docs.text: This page explains key concepts in plain language and gives practical standards that work in most engineering projects.

docs.title: Why Concepts Matter
docs.text:
• Tools change.
  • Menus change.
  • Good concepts stay useful.

If concepts are clear, practical execution becomes faster and more accurate.

docs.title: Raster
docs.text:
Definition: A raster is an image made of many grid cells called pixels. Each pixel stores one value.

Context: Raster is used when data is continuous, such as elevation, temperature, and imagery.

Usage: - Satellite basemap. - DEM. - Slope and terrain products.

Pros: - Strong for terrain and surface analysis. - Easy to visualize as continuous surface.

Cons: - Large file size. - Precision depends on pixel size.

Recommendation: Use raster for terrain and background context, not for precise engineering boundaries.

docs.title: Vector
docs.text:
Definition: Vector data stores shapes as coordinates.

Types: - Point. - Line. - Polygon.

Context: Vector is used for assets and boundaries.

Usage: - Survey points. - Road centerlines. - Building outlines. - Plot boundaries.

Pros: - Precise geometry. - Easy attribute management. - Smaller files for many use cases.

Cons: - Less suitable for continuous surfaces like elevation.

Recommendation: Use vector for design geometry and reporting layers.

docs.title: Coordinate Reference System (CRS)
docs.text:
Definition: CRS is a rule set that tells software how coordinates relate to real-world locations.

Key CRS used in this reference: - EPSG:4326, latitude and longitude in degrees. - EPSG:3857, common web map display CRS. - EPSG:32643, projected UTM CRS suited for metric engineering work in the target region.

Practical rule: - Use EPSG:4326 for KML and broad data exchange. - Use EPSG:32643 for distance, area, and slope analysis. - Use EPSG:3857 only for web background viewing.

docs.title: Attribute Data
docs.text:
Definition: Attributes are table fields attached to map features.

Common fields: - ID. - Code. - Elevation. - Description.

Recommendation: - Keep field names short and clear. - Avoid spaces in field names for compatibility. - Keep units explicit, for example Elev_m.

docs.title: File Formats and Their Role
docs.text:
• CSV: simple tabular exchange, excellent for survey points and schedules.
  • GeoTIFF: georeferenced raster format for basemap and DEM.
  • Shapefile: widely supported vector format.
  • GeoPackage: modern single-file vector and raster container.
  • KML or KMZ: earth browser visualization and sharing.
  • DWG or DXF: CAD drafting and design exchange.

Recommendation: Use GeoPackage when possible for editing workflows, and export Shapefile or KML only when needed by external users.

docs.title: Industry Standards and Practical Conventions
docs.text:
• Follow OGC-aligned geospatial exchange where possible.
  • Keep CRS metadata correct at each import and export step.
  • Store elevation units clearly.
  • Use clear layer naming.
  • Keep one source of truth for each dataset.

docs.title: Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Building and small campus planning.
  • Approach road concept and slope checks.
  • Survey point visualization and conversion.
  • Contour generation from DEM.
  • CAD to GIS and GIS to CAD transfer.

docs.title: Screenshot Placeholder

docs.title: Dataset Templates
docs.text: This page provides starter files for practical execution.

docs.title: What Is Included
docs.text:
• Survey CSV sample.
  • AOI sample polygon.
  • Approach road sample line.
  • Building footprint sample polygon.
  • CAD layer template table.
  • AutoCAD script template for quick two-room draft.

docs.title: Download and Use
docs.text:
• ts_points_sample.csv
  • aoi_sample.geojson
  • approach_road_sample.geojson
  • building_footprint_sample.geojson
  • cad_layers_template.csv
  • cad_two_room_template.scr
  • README.md

docs.title: How To Adapt for Any Location
docs.text:
1. Replace AOI geometry with your project polygon.
  2. Replace survey CSV points with project observations.
  3. Keep column names consistent to avoid import issues.
  4. Keep CRS metadata documented in file notes.

docs.title: Recommended Data Hygiene
docs.text:
• Keep original raw files unchanged.
  • Use separate cleaned versions.
  • Keep units in field names when needed.
  • Keep IDs unique and stable.

docs.title: Excel 365 Reference
docs.text: This page focuses on high-impact spreadsheet workflows for engineering data.

docs.title: What Excel Is
docs.text:
Definition: Excel is a spreadsheet tool used for calculation, tabular data management, validation, and reporting.

Context: It is the fastest way to clean and verify survey or quantity data before CAD or GIS use.

Typical use cases: - Room and opening schedules. - Survey point checks. - Quantity calculations. - Lookup and data standardization.

docs.title: Why It Is Useful
docs.text:
Pros: - Fast calculations. - Strong filtering and sorting. - Easy duplicate and unique checks. - Good print setup for reports.

Cons: - Manual editing can introduce mistakes. - Formula references can break if structure is poor. - Weak metadata compared to GIS databases.

Recommendation: Use structured tables and clear column naming from the start.

docs.title: Core Concepts in Simple Terms
docs.text:
• Row: one record.
  • Column: one field.
  • Cell: intersection of row and column.
  • Formula: rule that calculates a value.
  • Table: structured range with filter and sort controls.

docs.title: High-Impact Operations
docs.text:
• Basic math operations.
  • round, mround, ceil, floor.
  • index-match lookup.
  • Duplicate detection.
  • unique value extraction.
  • find and replace.
  • Convert range to table.
  • Filter, sort, and quick stats.
  • Page setup for print.

docs.title: Part A: Import and Clean Survey CSV
docs.text:
1. Open CSV in Excel.
  2. Verify delimiter, decimal format, and headers.
  3. Convert range to table.
  4. Standardize columns such as PointID, Easting, Northing, Elevation, Code.

docs.title: Part B: Validate Data
docs.text:
1. Check missing values.
  2. Detect duplicates.
  3. Generate unique list for point codes.
  4. Use find and replace for naming consistency.

docs.title: Part C: Build Engineering Sheets
docs.text:
1. Create room schedule table.
  2. Create opening schedule table.
  3. Apply basic math for totals.
  4. Use round and mround for report-ready values.
  5. Use ceil or floor where rule-based rounding is needed.
  6. Use index-match for category and rate mapping.

docs.title: Part D: Reporting and Sharing
docs.text:
1. Apply filters and sort for quick review.
  2. Set print area and page setup.
  3. Save in OneDrive synced folder.
  4. Share link and check version history.

docs.title: Practical Formula Notes
docs.text:
• Use round for standard decimal control.
  • Use mround for nearest specified step size.
  • Use ceil for conservative upper values.
  • Use floor for conservative lower values.
  • Use index-match when lookup column is not fixed.

docs.title: Industry Practices
docs.text:
• Keep one record per row.
  • Avoid merged cells in data tables.
  • Keep unit columns explicit.
  • Use data validation where repeated code values are expected.
  • Freeze header row for long tables.

docs.title: Recommendations for Beginner to Intermediate Users
docs.text:
• Keep formulas simple and readable.
  • Test formulas on 5 rows before applying to all rows.
  • Keep raw data in one sheet and calculated output in another sheet.
  • Keep a short data dictionary for column meanings.

docs.title: Interoperability Workflow
docs.text: This page explains how to move data safely between Excel, Civil 3D, QGIS, and Google Earth Pro.

docs.title: What Interoperability Means
docs.text:
Definition: Interoperability means one dataset can be used in another tool without losing meaning, geometry, scale, or important attributes.

Context: Engineering workflows often fail because files are exchanged without checking CRS, units, and field mappings.

docs.title: Why Interoperability Matters
docs.text:
Pros: - Reduces duplicate work. - Improves consistency across teams. - Enables faster communication with mixed software users.

Cons: - Wrong CRS can silently shift data. - Some formats drop style or attribute types. - Repeated conversion can degrade data quality.

Recommendation: Treat one file as source of truth and publish derived exports for other tools.

docs.title: End-to-End Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Total Station CSV] --> B[Excel Cleaning and Validation]
    A --> C[QGIS Delimited Text Layer]
    C --> D[GeoPackage or Shapefile]
    D --> E[Civil 3D Mapimport]
    C --> F[KML or KMZ]
    E --> G[Civil 3D Mapexport]
    G --> D
    H[QGIS Basemap GeoTIFF EPSG 32643] --> I[Civil 3D Mapiinsert]
    F --> J[Google Earth Pro]

docs.title: Decision Guide for Format Selection
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Exchange Data] --> B{What type of data?}
    B -->|Tabular points| C[CSV]
    B -->|Engineering vectors| D[GeoPackage or Shapefile]
    B -->|Earth browser view| E[KML or KMZ]
    B -->|Background raster| F[GeoTIFF]
    D --> G{Need CAD editing?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use mapimport in Civil 3D]
    G -->|No| I[Use directly in QGIS]

docs.title: Total Station CSV to Excel
docs.text:
1. Import CSV.
  2. Verify PointID, Easting, Northing, Elevation, and Code fields.
  3. Convert to table.
  4. Clean duplicates and missing values.
  5. Save a cleaned version.

docs.title: Total Station CSV to QGIS
docs.text:
1. Add delimited text layer.
  2. Set X and Y fields.
  3. Assign source CRS correctly.
  4. Save as GeoPackage for editing stability.

docs.title: QGIS Basemap to Civil 3D
docs.text:
1. Download basemap in QGIS.
  2. Reproject raster to EPSG:32643 with Warp GDAL.
  3. Insert into Civil 3D with mapiinsert.
  4. Verify alignment using known points.

docs.title: QGIS Vector to Civil 3D and Back
docs.text:
1. Export vector from QGIS to Shapefile or GeoPackage.
  2. Import in Civil 3D using mapimport.
  3. Edit and annotate as required.
  4. Export GIS-ready vector using mapexport.
  5. Validate in QGIS.

docs.title: KML or KMZ for Google Earth Pro
docs.text:
1. Export final communication layer from QGIS as KML or KMZ.
  2. Open in Google Earth Pro.
  3. Verify location, labels, and geometry.

docs.title: Industry Standards and Good Practice
docs.text:
• Keep CRS metadata explicit in every file handoff.
  • Use OGC-friendly formats where possible.
  • Keep unit fields explicit for elevation and distance.
  • Maintain clear naming and version labels.
  • Avoid unnecessary repeated format conversion.

docs.title: Screenshot Placeholders
docs.text:

Insert screenshot: mapimport settings and imported geometry in Civil 3D.

Insert screenshot: reprojected basemap inserted in Civil 3D using mapiinsert.

docs.title: Practical Execution Guide
docs.text: This guide gives one connected workflow from raw data to final outputs.

docs.title: Project Scenario
docs.text: Use a simple project: - Two-room building, ground floor only. - One short approach road. - Total station points in CSV. - AOI for basemap and DEM.

docs.title: Expected Outputs
docs.text:
• CAD drawing with dimensions and annotations.
  • Plot PDF.
  • Excel workbook with cleaned tables and formulas.
  • Reprojected basemap GeoTIFF.
  • Reprojected DEM GeoTIFF.
  • Contour vector layer.
  • KML or KMZ for Google Earth Pro.

docs.title: Workflow Overview
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Prepare Dataset] --> B[Clean CSV in Excel]
    B --> C[Draft Building in Civil 3D]
    C --> D[Create Layout and Plot PDF]
    A --> E[Process AOI in QGIS]
    E --> F[Download Basemap and DEM]
    F --> G[Reproject to EPSG 32643]
    G --> H[Extract Contours]
    H --> I[Map Export]
    C --> J[CAD GIS Export]
    I --> K[KML or KMZ]
    J --> K
    K --> L[View in Google Earth Pro]

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare Dataset Folder
docs.text:
1. Create folders for CAD, Excel, GIS, and exports.
  2. Copy starter CSV and GeoJSON files.
  3. Keep original files unchanged as backup.

docs.title: Step 2: Process Survey CSV in Excel
docs.text:
1. Open CSV.
  2. Verify PointID, Easting, Northing, Elevation, and Code columns.
  3. Convert to table.
  4. Remove obvious duplicates.
  5. Create unique code list.
  6. Save cleaned file.

docs.title: Step 3: Build CAD Drawing in Civil 3D
docs.text:
1. Start drawing with clear layer setup.
  2. Draw two-room ground-floor plan with polyline.
  3. Add wall thickness using offset.
  4. Create door and window openings using trim and extend.
  5. Create one simple section.
  6. Create one simple elevation.
  7. Add dimensions and annotations.
  8. Create one layout and export PDF.

docs.title: Step 4: Prepare Basemap and DEM in QGIS
docs.text:
1. Set project CRS.
  2. Load satellite map from QuickMapServices.
  3. Download AOI basemap using Advanced Map Downloader.
  4. Download Copernicus DEM from OpenTopography DEM Downloader.
  5. Reproject both rasters to EPSG:32643 with Warp GDAL.

docs.title: Step 5: Generate Contours and Basic Map
docs.text:
1. Run GDAL Contour on reprojected DEM.
  2. Select practical contour interval.
  3. Style contours with readable labels.
  4. Build map layout with title, legend, north arrow, and scale bar.
  5. Export PDF.

docs.title: Step 6: Exchange Data Across Tools
docs.text:
1. Import cleaned CSV to QGIS as points.
  2. Export selected layer to shapefile or GeoPackage.
  3. Import vector into Civil 3D using mapimport.
  4. Insert georeferenced raster in Civil 3D using mapiinsert.
  5. Export CAD output for GIS using mapexport.
  6. Convert final vector to KML or KMZ.
  7. Open in Google Earth Pro.

docs.title: Step 7: Save and Share with OneDrive
docs.text:
1. Save project files to OneDrive synced folder.
  2. Keep file names version-friendly.
  3. Use version history after major edits.
  4. Share read-only and edit links as required.

docs.title: Final Quality Checks
docs.text:
• Geometry is clean and readable.
  • Units and CRS are consistent.
  • Tables have no duplicate critical IDs.
  • Map and CAD outputs align spatially.
  • Final exports open in target tools.

docs.title: Screenshot Placeholders
docs.text:
Insert screenshot: integrated project folder with all output files.

Insert screenshot: final CAD plot and GIS map side by side.

docs.title: QGIS and Google Earth Pro Reference
docs.text: This page provides simple but complete guidance for GIS theory and practical execution.

docs.title: What QGIS Is
docs.text:
Definition: QGIS is an open-source Geographic Information System used to view, edit, analyze, and export spatial data.

Context: It is used for basemap preparation, terrain processing, contour extraction, and map publication.

Typical use cases: - Basemap download and preparation. - DEM processing and contour generation. - Vector editing and conversion. - Map layout and export.

docs.title: What Google Earth Pro Is
docs.text:
Definition: Google Earth Pro is a visualization tool that displays geospatial data on a globe-based earth browser.

Context: It is best for communication and quick location understanding, not detailed engineering analysis.

docs.title: Why These Tools Are Useful
docs.text:
Pros: - Strong raster and vector support. - Wide format compatibility. - Powerful processing tools. - Good map export options.

Cons: - CRS mismatch is a common error source. - Plugin quality can vary. - Heavy rasters can slow performance.

Recommendation: Keep one consistent processing CRS and verify at every import step.

docs.title: Raster and Vector
docs.text:
• Raster stores values in pixels.
  • Vector stores geometry as points, lines, and polygons.

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text:
• Attributes are table columns linked to features.
  • Correct field type prevents sorting and calculation issues.

docs.title: CRS Basics
docs.text:
• EPSG:4326 for lat-long and KML workflows.
  • EPSG:3857 for web basemap display.
  • EPSG:32643 for metric analysis and engineering context.

docs.title: High-Impact Plugins
docs.text:
• QuickWKT for coordinate checks.
  • NextGIS QuickMapServices for quick basemap loading.
  • Advanced Map Downloader for AOI basemap download.
  • OpenTopography DEM Downloader for Copernicus DEM.
  • Profile Tool for elevation profiles.
  • Road Slope Calculator for route slope checks.

docs.title: Part A: Basemap Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open project and set project CRS.
  2. Load satellite basemap with QuickMapServices.
  3. Download AOI basemap using Advanced Map Downloader.
  4. Save output as GeoTIFF.
  5. Reproject to EPSG:32643 using Warp GDAL.

docs.title: Part B: DEM Workflow
docs.text:
1. Download Copernicus Global DEM 30 m using OpenTopography DEM Downloader.
  2. Save DEM raster.
  3. Reproject DEM to EPSG:32643 with Warp GDAL.

docs.title: Part C: Contour Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open GDAL Contour tool.
  2. Select DEM as input.
  3. Set contour interval.
  4. Save contour output as vector layer.
  5. Apply simple contour style and labels.

docs.title: Part D: Conversion and Export Workflow
docs.text:
1. Convert shapefile to KML or KMZ when needed.
  2. Convert KML or KMZ back to shapefile when needed.
  3. Prepare map layout with title, legend, north arrow, and scale bar.
  4. Export map as PDF.
  5. Open KML or KMZ in Google Earth Pro for communication view.

docs.title: Industry Practices
docs.text:
• Reproject analysis rasters and vectors to projected CRS before measurement.
  • Keep no-data values consistent.
  • Record contour interval in layer name.
  • Keep style files with data for reproducibility.
  • Export final communication layers in KML or KMZ for wider access.

docs.title: Recommendations for Beginner to Intermediate Users
docs.text:
• Keep project CRS fixed once processing starts.
  • Use nearest rules for categorical rasters and bilinear for imagery where appropriate.
  • Start with basic symbology and improve only if needed.
  • Validate outputs with one known control point.

docs.title: Screenshot Placeholders
docs.text:

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Use this page when outputs are not matching expected results.

docs.title: Civil 3D
docs.text:
• Objects not visible after zoom: Use zoom extents and check layer on or off state.
  • Wrong plot output: Check viewport scale, lineweight display, and plot area.
  • Imported GIS data shifted: Verify drawing units and coordinate system assumptions.
  • Raster not aligning: Check georeferencing and CRS before mapiinsert.

docs.title: Excel
docs.text:
• Wrong formula results: Check numeric format and decimal separator.
  • index-match not returning value: Check exact key match and remove hidden spaces.
  • Duplicate detection issues: Normalize text case and trim spaces first.
  • Print output broken: Reset print area and verify page orientation.

docs.title: QGIS
docs.text:
• Layer appears in wrong location: Check source CRS at import and project CRS.
  • Warp output empty or wrong: Verify input raster validity and target CRS EPSG 32643.
  • Contour not generated: Confirm DEM has valid elevation values and no-data settings.
  • KML output misplaced in Google Earth: Verify export CRS and geometry type.

docs.title: OneDrive
docs.text:
• Sync conflict files created: Keep latest file, archive conflict copy, and continue.
  • Shared file not visible to reviewer: Verify link permissions and account sign-in.

docs.title: Starter Dataset Package
docs.text: This folder contains simple starter files for practice.

docs.title: Included Files
docs.text:
• ts_points_sample.csv
  • aoi_sample.geojson
  • approach_road_sample.geojson
  • building_footprint_sample.geojson
  • cad_layers_template.csv
  • cad_two_room_template.scr

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Coordinate reference for GeoJSON files is EPSG:4326.
  • CSV Easting and Northing are sample projected values for practice.
  • Convert GeoJSON to shapefile in QGIS if required by legacy workflows.
  • Use the AutoCAD script as a starting template, then edit as needed.
